name: From chats to systems
tagline: The third time you type the same brief into a chat box, don't type it — build it. This module climbs the short ladder from a chat you keep repeating, to a saved brief, to a playbook (a repeatable recipe with a trigger, steps, and a place the output lands), to an agent (a described team member that handles varied work in one lane). Rule of thumb: same steps every time is a playbook; judgment inside a lane is an agent; and a person still approves the result until the next module says otherwise.
overview: You have been getting good results in chat (Module 02) and the assistant knows your business (Module 03). Now you notice you are re-typing the same instructions every week. This module is about stopping that. It teaches three rungs in plain words: a SAVED BRIEF is your Module 02 delegation brief, kept and reused instead of retyped; a PLAYBOOK is that brief turned into a repeatable recipe — a trigger (on demand, on a schedule, or when an event happens), a short list of steps, and an output that lands where your work actually happens; an AGENT is a described team member with a job, the knowledge base you built in Module 03, and a specific set of tools it is allowed to use, which handles varied work inside its lane rather than one fixed recipe. The honest choice between them: same steps every time means a playbook; judgment within a lane means an agent; and the safe default is playbook first, agent only when the playbook keeps needing exceptions. Throughout, a human approves every output — the next module (Delegating with guardrails) decides, deliberately, what earns more autonomy. The module ends with you shipping one real weekly task as a scheduled playbook and running it twice. This is orientation for a business owner, not a platform-mastery course; the deep platform curriculum is a separate track if you want it later.
objectives: Recognise the signal that a chat should become a system — the moment you have typed the same brief for the third time — and name the three rungs of the ladder (saved brief, playbook, agent).
Describe a playbook at owner level: a trigger (on demand, on a schedule, or on an event), an ordered list of steps, and an output that lands where the work happens.
Describe an agent at owner level: a role, the knowledge it draws on, the tools it is allowed to use, and its boundaries — a described team member, not a script.
Choose honestly between a playbook and an agent using the rule of thumb (same steps every time versus judgment within a lane) and apply the playbook-first default.

Explain why a person still approves every output at this stage, and name the three common mistakes: staying in chat forever, jumping to a do-everything agent, and automating a process you have not fixed.

lessons.title: The signal, and the ladder off the chat box
lessons.objective: Spot the moment a chat should become a system, and place the three rungs — saved brief, playbook, agent — in order.
lessons.body:
## The signal: you have typed this before

Here is the moment this module is about. It is Friday afternoon. You open a chat and type, more or less, the same thing you typed last Friday: *"Read this week's supplier emails, work out what we're low on, and draft the order sheet in our usual format."* You paste in the emails. You tweak the result. You send it. Next Friday, you do it again.

That feeling — *I have typed this before* — is the signal. In Module 02 you learned to write a good delegation brief and get a clean result in chat. That is the right tool for a one-off. But a chat is a conversation: it starts empty every time, and you carry the instructions in your head and re-type them. The third Friday you type the same brief, you are not really having a conversation any more. You are running a process by hand. Processes should not be run by hand.

> Plain-English rule of thumb: **if you could hand someone a written recipe and they could follow it, it should not live in a chat box.** Chat is for thinking and one-offs. Repeated work belongs in a system.

## The ladder: three rungs, each a bit more built

Getting off the chat box is not one big leap. It is a short ladder, and most small businesses only need the first two rungs.

**Rung 1 — a saved brief.** This is the smallest step, and it costs nothing. Take the delegation brief you keep re-typing and *save it* — in a note, a document, anywhere you can copy it from. Now instead of reconstructing the instructions from memory each Friday, you paste a known-good brief and just add this week's emails. You have removed the retyping and the drift (the small differences that creep in when you rebuild instructions from memory). It is still you, in chat, doing it — but faster and more consistently.

**Rung 2 — a playbook.** A **playbook** is that saved brief turned into a repeatable recipe the platform runs for you. In Automatos, a playbook is a multi-step workflow: you define the steps once, then run them on demand or on a schedule. Instead of *"every Friday I open chat and paste the brief,"* the recipe itself says: on Friday at 4pm, read this week's orders, draft the weekly summary, and file it here. You are no longer the one pressing the keys. You are the one who checks the result. This is the rung most repeated tasks should settle on.

**Rung 3 — an agent.** An **agent** is a described team member — a worker with a job, the knowledge you gave it in Module 03, and a specific set of tools it is allowed to use. You reach for an agent when the work is not one fixed recipe but *varied work inside one lane*: not only "draft the Friday order," but also "are we low on oat milk?" on a Tuesday, and "which supplier was cheapest last month?" whenever you ask. A playbook runs the same steps; an agent uses judgment within its lane. More on the honest choice between them in Lesson 3 — for now, just know the agent is the top rung, and you rarely start there.

> The important idea: these rungs build on each other. Your saved brief becomes the steps of your playbook. Your Module 03 knowledge base becomes what an agent knows. Nothing is wasted; you are climbing, not restarting.

## Worked example: the café's Friday supplier order

Meet the café — the business we will follow through this module. Every Friday the owner spends about forty minutes on the supplier order: reading the week's supplier emails, checking what ran low, and typing up the order sheet in their usual format. Today it is forty minutes of chat-and-paste.

- **Rung 1:** the owner saves the exact brief they keep re-typing ("read these supplier emails, list what we're low on, draft the order in our format"). Friday now takes fifteen minutes, because they paste the brief instead of rebuilding it.
- **Rung 2:** the owner turns that brief into a scheduled playbook — every Friday at 4pm, draft the order sheet and file it in the right place. Friday now takes five minutes: the owner reads the draft, fixes anything off, and approves.
- **Rung 3 (later, only if needed):** if mid-week questions keep coming up — *"are we low on oat milk?"*, *"did the milk price go up?"* — that is judgment the fixed recipe cannot do. That is the signal to promote the task to a **supplies agent** that handles the whole lane, not just the Friday draft.

The café will not jump straight to Rung 3. It will ship the playbook first, run it twice, and only climb higher if the work actually demands judgment. That restraint is the whole lesson.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What is the everyday signal that a chat task should become a built system?

options.text: You notice you have typed the same brief into chat several times — you are running a process by hand
The assistant gave you a wrong answer once, so you should automate it away
The task is important, so it automatically needs an agent
You have never done the task before and want the system to figure it out

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The signal is repetition — retyping the same brief means you are running a process by hand, and processes belong in a system rather than a chat box. A single wrong answer is a reason to improve the brief, not to automate. Importance alone does not decide the tool, and a task you have never done and not yet fixed is exactly the wrong thing to automate — you fix it first (that is one of the module's named mistakes).

knowledgeCheck.stem: Put the three rungs of the ladder in the order the module climbs them.

options.text: Saved brief → playbook → agent
Agent → playbook → saved brief
Playbook → saved brief → agent
Saved brief → agent → playbook

knowledgeCheck.explanation: You climb from the cheapest, smallest step upward: a saved brief (keep and reuse the instructions), then a playbook (turn the brief into a repeatable recipe the platform runs), then — only if the work needs judgment in a lane — an agent. Starting at the agent, or putting the playbook before the saved brief, skips the steps that make each rung easy; and 'saved brief → agent → playbook' jumps past the playbook rung most tasks should settle on.

lessons.title: Anatomy of a playbook: trigger, steps, output
lessons.objective: Describe a playbook at owner level and set its three moving parts — when it runs, what it does, and where the result lands.
lessons.body:
## A playbook, in plain words

A **playbook** is a recipe the platform runs for you. In Automatos, it is a multi-step workflow: you define the steps once, then run them on demand or on a schedule. If you have ever written down "first do this, then this, then file it there" so someone else could follow it, you have already written the shape of a playbook.

Three parts are worth understanding as an owner. You do not need to know how they are built under the hood — you need to know what decision each one is asking you to make.

### 1. The trigger — *when does it run?*

The trigger is the "when." A playbook can run three ways:

- **On demand** — you press **Run** when you want it. Good while you are still checking the output every time.
- **On a schedule** — it runs by itself at a set time. Automatos uses cron schedules (a standard way of writing "every weekday at 9am" or "Sundays at 10pm") set in the playbook's trigger settings. This is the rung most weekly tasks want: *every Friday at 4pm, do the thing.*
- **On an event** — something happens in a connected app and the playbook runs in response. The documented examples are developer-flavoured (a new GitHub pull request, a new Jira issue, a Slack message), but the idea is general: *when this happens, run that.*

> Start on demand, move to a schedule. Run it by hand a couple of times, confirm the output is right, then let the schedule take over. Event triggers are the advanced case — reach for them later, only when a clear event marks the moment the work should start.

### 2. The steps — *what does it do?*

The steps are the recipe itself: an ordered list of tasks, each handled by an agent. A step reads something, drafts something, checks something, or files something. Later steps can use what earlier steps produced — Automatos passes each step's output down the line so step 3 can build on step 2.

The platform's own worked example is a nightly code review: pull the latest commits, review them, run a security scan, write a summary, post it to the team. That is developer work, but the *shape* is exactly your Friday order: **read → work out → draft → file**. Keep your steps few and boring. A playbook that does one thing excellently beats one that tries to do everything.

### 3. The output — *where does the result land?*

The last question is the one people forget: where does the finished work go? A playbook's output should land where your work actually happens — a document filed in the right place, a draft email waiting to be sent, a message in the right channel. The point of climbing off the chat box is that you stop copying results out of a chat by hand; the playbook delivers to the destination for you.

## Where you watch it run

Once a playbook is scheduled, you do not lose sight of it. Automatos shows scheduled and running playbooks on the **Activity → Dashboard** — a weekly calendar marks the days a routine runs, and an *Active Now* section shows anything in progress with a step count like "Step 2/4." (A **routine** is simply the platform's word for a *scheduled* playbook.) You will use this surface in the hands-on step to confirm your playbook actually ran.

## Worked example: the marketing agency's Monday report

The marketing agency sends every client a short Monday performance note. Today an account manager rebuilds each one from a chat, pasting in the week's numbers. As a playbook it becomes concrete:

- **Trigger:** on a schedule — every Monday at 8am. (They will run it on demand the first couple of times to check it, then switch on the schedule.)
- **Steps:** read this week's figures → draft the client note in the agency's standard format → file the draft where the account manager can review it.
- **Output:** the draft lands in the shared drafts folder, one per client, ready for a human to glance over and send.

Notice what did *not* change: a person still reads and approves every note before it reaches a client. The playbook removed the retyping, not the judgement. Keeping that approval is the subject we return to in Lesson 3 — and it stays on until the next module deliberately decides otherwise.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A playbook has three owner-level parts. Which trio is it?

options.text: A trigger (when it runs), steps (what it does), and an output (where the result lands)
A model, a temperature setting, and a token limit
A persona, a boundary, and a budget
A username, a password, and a schedule

knowledgeCheck.explanation: At owner level a playbook is a trigger, steps, and an output — when it runs, what it does, and where the finished work goes. Model/temperature/token settings are agent tuning, not the shape of a playbook; persona and boundaries describe an agent (Lesson 3); and a username/password is account setup, not a workflow definition.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You want a weekly task to run by itself every Friday at 4pm. Which trigger fits, and what is the sensible way to get there?

options.text: A schedule (a cron trigger) — but run it on demand a couple of times first to check the output, then switch the schedule on
An event trigger, because 'every Friday' is an event in a connected app
On demand only — scheduled runs are not supported in playbooks
A schedule, switched on immediately with no manual test, since testing wastes time

knowledgeCheck.explanation: 'Every Friday at 4pm' is a time-based schedule, which Automatos sets with a cron trigger — and the sensible path is to run it on demand first, confirm the result, then let the schedule take over. A recurring clock time is not an app event (event triggers respond to things like a new pull request), scheduled runs certainly are supported, and switching a schedule on with no manual test is exactly the kind of shortcut that ships a broken routine.

lessons.title: Agent or playbook? The honest choice, and why a human still approves
lessons.objective: Describe an agent at owner level, choose between an agent and a playbook using the rule of thumb, and explain why approval stays on and which mistakes to avoid.
lessons.body:
## An agent, in plain words

An **agent** is a described team member — a worker, not a chatbot. When you create one in Automatos you give it four things, and they map neatly onto how you would hire a person:

- **A role** — a clear description of the job. "Reviews code for JavaScript projects" beats "code stuff." (The platform even uses this description to route the right requests to the right agent, so specificity pays off twice.)
- **Knowledge** — what it draws on to do the job well. This is the knowledge base you built in Module 03: your products, your policies, your standard formats.
- **Tools** — the specific external apps it is *allowed* to use (connected through the Tools page). An agent can only use a tool you have assigned to it — nothing more.
- **Boundaries** — what it should and should not do. In practice these live in the agent's instructions: "focus on X, do not do Y." A worker with a job description and limits, not a loose cannon.

The difference from a playbook is the kind of work it handles. A playbook runs *the same steps every time*. An agent handles *varied work inside one lane* — you can ask it different questions on different days and it uses judgment to answer within its role. The café's Friday-order playbook drafts one thing on a schedule; a café *supplies agent* would also answer "are we low on oat milk?" on a Tuesday and "which supplier was cheapest last month?" whenever asked.

## The rule of thumb

Here is the honest way to choose, and it fits on a sticky note:

> **Same steps every time → playbook. Judgment within a lane → agent.**

If you can write the work down as a fixed recipe that would run the same way each time, it is a playbook. If the work needs someone to decide *what to do this time* — different inputs, different questions, a judgment call inside a defined area — it is an agent.

And there is a default that keeps you out of trouble: **playbook first; promote to an agent only when the playbook keeps needing exceptions.** A boring playbook that runs perfectly is worth more than an ambitious agent you do not yet trust. If you find yourself constantly stepping in to handle cases the recipe cannot, that is the signal the work actually needs judgment — and that is when you climb to the agent rung. Do that only after the playbook has run at least twice, so you are promoting from evidence, not enthusiasm.

## A human still approves — for now

At this stage, whatever you build, **a person approves the output before it counts.** The Friday order draft is reviewed before it is sent. The Monday client note is read before a client sees it. You are the checkpoint.

This is deliberate, not timidity. The next module — Delegating with guardrails — is entirely about deciding, on purpose, what a system has earned the right to do on its own, and what safety rails go around it. Until you have worked through that, the safe posture is simple: automate the *typing*, keep the *judgement*. Approval stays on.

## Three mistakes to avoid

Most people who try to climb this ladder trip on one of three things:

1. **Staying in chat forever.** You keep re-typing the brief because building the playbook feels like effort. It is less effort than the fiftieth manual Friday. Climb the first rung.
2. **Jumping straight to a do-everything agent.** A single agent meant to run your whole business is hard to describe, hard to trust, and hard to debug. One boring, excellent playbook beats a sprawling agent every time. Earn the agent.
3. **Automating a process you have not fixed.** If the task is messy, unclear, or a bit broken when *you* do it, automating it just makes a mess faster and more reliably. Fix the process by hand first; then build it.

## Worked examples: two shops, two right answers

The **e-commerce shop** wants its weekly restock report: read the week's sales, list what is running low, draft the reorder sheet. Same steps, every week, same shape → **playbook.** There is no judgment call that changes week to week; there is a recipe. Ship it as a scheduled playbook, approve the draft, done.

The **consultancy** wants help answering the varied client questions that land in its inbox — scoping a new request one day, pulling last quarter's numbers the next, drafting a follow-up after that. No two are the same, but they all sit in one lane (client support) and all draw on the same knowledge. That is judgment within a lane → **agent.** But even the consultancy starts lower: it would first build playbooks for the repeatable pieces (the standard follow-up, the standard status update) and only stand up an agent once it is clear the recipes keep hitting cases they cannot handle. Playbook first, agent when the playbook keeps needing exceptions — and a person signs off either way, until the next module says otherwise.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A café task always follows the identical steps — read supplier emails, list what's low, draft the order in the usual format — and never needs a different decision week to week. Playbook or agent, and why?

options.text: A playbook — the steps are the same every time, which is exactly what a playbook is for
An agent, because supplier orders are important business
An agent, because it involves reading emails and any email task needs judgment
Neither — a task this repetitive should stay in chat so you keep control

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Same steps every time is the definition of a playbook, so that is the right rung. Importance does not decide the tool — plenty of important work is a fixed recipe. Reading emails is not automatically a judgment call when the steps never change. And keeping a fully repeatable task in chat is the 'staying in chat forever' mistake — approval keeps you in control without the retyping.

knowledgeCheck.stem: At this stage of the course, what happens to a playbook's or agent's output before it counts?

options.text: A person reviews and approves it — you are the checkpoint until the next module deliberately decides what earns autonomy
It is sent or acted on automatically the moment it is produced
It is deleted unless you upgrade to a paid plan
It is approved automatically as long as the playbook ran on schedule

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The whole posture right now is 'automate the typing, keep the judgement' — a human approves every output, and the next module (Delegating with guardrails) is where autonomy is granted on purpose. Nothing acts on its own yet, output is not deleted behind a paywall, and running on schedule does not remove the human check — the schedule automates when it runs, not whether a person signs off.

questions.stem: In plain terms, what is a playbook in Automatos?

options.text: A multi-step workflow you define once and then run on demand or on a schedule
A single chat message you send to the assistant
A document you upload so the assistant knows your business
The paid subscription tier that unlocks automation

questions.explanation: A playbook is a repeatable, multi-step workflow — defined once, run on demand or on a schedule. A one-off chat message is the thing you are trying to stop repeating; an uploaded document is your Module 03 knowledge base, not a workflow; and 'a paid tier' is not what a playbook is — it is a feature you use to turn repeated work into a recipe.

questions.stem: What is the smallest, cheapest first rung off the chat box — the one that costs nothing to try?

options.text: Saving the delegation brief you keep re-typing so you can paste it instead of rebuilding it from memory
Standing up a full agent with tools and a knowledge base
Building a scheduled playbook with event triggers
Connecting every app in the Tools page so nothing is missing

questions.explanation: The first rung is a saved brief — keep the instructions and reuse them, which removes the retyping and the drift at no cost. A full agent is the top rung you rarely start on; a scheduled playbook is the second rung and more work than just saving text; and connecting every app upfront is unnecessary — you connect the tools a task actually needs.

questions.stem: Which of these is the clearest case for an AGENT rather than a playbook?

options.text: Answering the varied questions that land in a client inbox — different each day, but all within one lane and drawing on the same knowledge
Drafting the identical weekly restock report from the week's sales, the same way every time
Filing a standard month-end summary in the same folder on the same date each month
Posting a fixed 'we're open' message to a channel every morning

questions.explanation: Varied work within one lane, decided case by case, is exactly what an agent is for — the client inbox fits because no two requests are the same yet they share a lane and a knowledge base. The other three are fixed recipes — same steps, same output, on a schedule — which is the definition of a playbook, not an agent.

questions.stem: What is the safe default when you are unsure whether to build a playbook or an agent?

options.text: Playbook first — promote to an agent only when the playbook keeps needing exceptions the recipe can't handle
Agent first — it is more capable, so it is always the better starting point
Build both at once and see which performs better
Neither — keep everything in chat until you are completely certain

questions.explanation: Playbook first is the default: a boring recipe that runs perfectly is worth more than an agent you do not yet trust, and the playbook's failures tell you honestly when the work actually needs judgment. Starting with an agent skips that evidence; building both wastes effort; and staying in chat forever is the very habit the module is helping you break.

questions.stem: When you build an agent, what set of tools can it actually use?

options.text: Only the tools you have connected in the workspace and explicitly assigned to that agent
Every one of the 1,000+ integrations, automatically, the moment it is created
Whatever tools it decides it needs, without you assigning anything
No tools at all — agents can only chat, never act in external apps

questions.explanation: An agent can use only the tools you have connected and then assigned to it — that assignment is how you keep it in a lane. It does not get every integration by default, it cannot grant itself tools you have not assigned, and it is not limited to chat: assigned tools are exactly what let an agent act in external apps.

questions.stem: A playbook step needs the result of the step before it. How does that work?

options.text: The platform passes each step's output down the line, so a later step can build on what an earlier step produced
It doesn't — every step starts fresh with no knowledge of earlier steps
You must copy the earlier step's result by hand and paste it into the next step
Only the very first step can produce anything; the rest just repeat it

questions.explanation: Steps are chained — the platform makes each step's output available to the next, which is what lets 'read → draft → file' work as one flow. Steps are not isolated from each other, you do not paste between them by hand (that would defeat the point of leaving the chat box), and every step can produce output, not just the first.

questions.stem: Which of these is one of the three classic mistakes this module warns against?

options.text: Automating a process that is still messy or unclear when you do it by hand
Running a new playbook on demand a few times before switching on its schedule
Keeping a human approval on the output while you build trust
Saving a good delegation brief so you can reuse it

questions.explanation: Automating an unfixed process just makes a mess faster — fix it by hand first, then build it. The other three are good practice, not mistakes: testing on demand before scheduling, keeping a human check on the output, and saving a reusable brief are all things the module actively recommends.

questions.stem: Once a playbook is scheduled, where in Automatos can you confirm it ran and see anything currently in progress?

options.text: The Activity → Dashboard — its schedule view marks days a routine runs, and an 'Active Now' section shows tasks in progress
You cannot — scheduled playbooks give no visibility once they are switched on
Only by re-running the playbook manually and watching the chat
In the billing settings, which is the only place runs are recorded

questions.explanation: The Activity Dashboard is the surface for this — a weekly schedule marks days with scheduled routines and 'Active Now' shows live progress with a step count. Scheduled playbooks are not invisible, you do not have to re-run them by hand to know they worked, and run history is an activity/analytics concern, not a billing one.

questions.stem: The café ships its Friday-order playbook, but over a few weeks the owner keeps stepping in mid-week to answer 'are we low on X?' and 'did this supplier's price change?' — questions the fixed recipe can't handle. What does this tell them?

options.text: The work has outgrown a fixed recipe and now needs judgment in a lane — it is time to consider promoting it to a supplies agent, now that the playbook has proven itself twice over
The playbook has failed and should be deleted, returning the whole task to manual chat
Nothing — you should never move beyond a playbook under any circumstances
They should have started with a do-everything agent from day one and skipped the playbook entirely

questions.explanation: Constant exceptions the recipe cannot handle are precisely the signal to climb to the agent rung — and having run the playbook well for weeks means the promotion is based on evidence, exactly as the default advises. The playbook did not fail; it did its job and revealed where judgment is needed. 'Never move beyond a playbook' ignores real judgment work, and starting with a do-everything agent is the mistake the playbook-first default exists to prevent.

labs.title: Ship one scheduled playbook — and run it twice
labs.goal: Take the repetitive weekly task you circled back in Module 01, and turn its Module 02 brief into a working Automatos playbook: a trigger, a few steps, and a place the output lands. Run it by hand, fix what's off, then let a weekly schedule take over — keeping yourself as the approver. The artifact is one real, scheduled playbook that has run at least twice.
labs.steps:
## Before you start

You are the Admin of your own personal workspace (Automatos creates one for you on first sign-in), so every step here is achievable solo. Have two things ready: the task you circled in Module 01 (a real, repetitive weekly task), and the delegation brief you wrote for it in Module 02. If a step in your task needs an external app — an email inbox, a document store, a chat channel — connect that app first from **Tools & Integrations** (it will ask you to sign in to the app and confirm permissions). Start read-only where you can; you can widen access later once you trust the result.

## What you'll do

1. **Write the recipe on paper first.** Before touching the platform, write your task as three or four plain steps: read *this*, work out *that*, draft *the thing*, file it *here*. If you cannot write it as clear steps, the task may not be ready to automate yet — fix the process by hand until you can. This paper recipe becomes your playbook's steps.
2. **Create the playbook.** Go to **Agents → Playbooks** and create a new playbook. Give it a clear name (e.g. "Friday supplier order") and add your steps in order, one line of instruction each. Keep it boring and few — one job, done well.
3. **Decide where the output lands.** Make the final step deliver the result to where your work actually happens — a draft in the right folder, a message in the right channel, a document filed in the right place. This is the part that gets you off copy-and-paste, so be specific about the destination.
4. **Run it on demand — twice.** Use **Run** to trigger the playbook by hand. Read the output as if a new hire produced it. Fix whatever is off — a vague step, a wrong destination, a missing detail — and run it again. Do not schedule anything until a manual run produces something you would actually approve.
5. **Set the weekly schedule.** In the playbook's trigger settings, set a schedule for your real cadence (start weekly — e.g. every Friday afternoon). Automatos uses cron schedules for this; a weekly pattern is the standard case. Save it.
6. **Confirm it's live and watch it run.** Open **Activity → Dashboard**. Check that your scheduled playbook (a *routine*) shows on the weekly schedule view. When it next runs, the *Active Now* section will show it in progress; afterwards, confirm the output landed where you told it to.
7. **Keep approval on.** You are still the checkpoint. Review every output before it is sent or acted on — automate the typing, keep the judgement. (The next module is where you decide, on purpose, what earns more autonomy.)
8. **Notice the promotion signal — but don't act on it yet.** If, over your two runs, you keep wishing the playbook could make a judgment call it can't ("but this week is different because..."), note it. That is your future signal to consider an agent — and the rule is to do that only after the playbook has run at least twice, which you will just have done.

## Why it matters

This is the module's whole point made real: you have taken a task you used to run by hand in a chat box and turned it into a system that runs itself and delivers where you work — with you still approving the result. That single shipped, scheduled, twice-run playbook is the 'one automated workflow' artifact the track is building toward.
checklist: The repetitive weekly task from Module 01 is written as three or four clear steps before building anything
A named playbook exists in Agents → Playbooks with those steps in order
The final step delivers the output to a specific real destination (folder, channel, or document) — not back into a chat
The playbook has been run on demand at least twice, with fixes applied between runs
A weekly schedule (cron trigger) is set and saved
The scheduled routine is visible on the Activity → Dashboard, and its output was confirmed to land in the right place
A human still approves the output before it is sent or acted on
Any 'this needs judgment the recipe can't do' moments are noted as a future agent-promotion signal (not acted on yet)

resources.title: Automatos docs — Playbooks (Agents → Playbooks)
resources.annotation: The platform's own definition of a playbook: a multi-step workflow you define once and run on demand, on a schedule (cron), or from chat. Read this for the trigger/steps/schedule structure, the worked 'nightly code review' example, how each step's output feeds the next, and how a completed mission can be saved as a reusable playbook ('Save as Routine').

resources.title: Automatos docs — Agents (Agent Management)
resources.annotation: What an agent is in the platform's words: a specialised AI worker with its own model, persona, tools, and skills — a worker, not a chatbot. Read this for the four things you give an agent (role/description, knowledge, tools, boundaries) and why a clear description matters (the router uses it). Grounds the 'agent = described team member' framing and the playbook-versus-agent choice.

resources.title: Automatos docs — Creating an Agent
resources.annotation: The step-by-step of standing up an agent: name and description, model, persona, and — the parts this module leans on — assigning tools and setting boundaries in the system prompt ('be specific about the role', 'set boundaries: what the agent should NOT do'). Useful when a task graduates from a playbook to an agent.

resources.title: Automatos docs — Tools & Integrations
resources.annotation: How agents reach external apps: connect an app, assign it to agents, and the tool router calls it when relevant. Read this for the connect→assign→use flow, the cron-scheduling section (how recurring playbook triggers are written), and the note that event/webhook triggers are configured per playbook. Grounds the 'trigger' and 'tools an agent is allowed to use' parts of the module.

resources.title: Automatos docs — Assigning Tools to Agents
resources.annotation: The key containment rule for this module: an agent can use only the tools you assign to it, and each assignment has a permission scope — Full access, Read only, or Custom (specific actions). The docs advise starting read-only for new agents. This is what keeps an agent inside its lane.

resources.title: Automatos docs — Coordination (how agents work together)
resources.annotation: How multi-step, multi-agent work shares context: each step sees the previous step's output through the workflow pipeline. Also documents delegation rules (Auto-delegate / Approval required / Disabled) — the 'approval required' setting is the platform-level echo of this module's 'a human still approves' posture, explored properly in the next module.

resources.title: Automatos docs — Activity Dashboard
resources.annotation: Where you watch a scheduled playbook (a 'routine') actually run: a weekly calendar marks days with scheduled routines, 'Active Now' shows tasks in progress with a step count, and the view updates in real time. This is the surface the hands-on step uses to confirm the playbook ran and its output landed.

resources.title: Automatos docs — Marketplace Playbooks
resources.annotation: If you would rather start from a ready-made recipe than a blank one: the community marketplace has pre-built playbooks you can install (they reference agent roles and get mapped to your own agents). Installed playbooks are independent copies scoped to your workspace — a useful shortcut, though most operator tasks are quick to build from scratch. (Community-shared and free; there is no paid or creator-earns marketplace.)

resources.title: Google — Generative AI Leader exam guide (business-strategy sections)
resources.annotation: Vendor-light, business-toned reference for leaders deciding where and how to apply generative AI. The 'AI solutions' and 'business strategy' sections reinforce this module's core judgment — when to encode a task as a repeatable workflow versus a more autonomous helper — without platform-specific detail. This is also the honest 'prove it' bridge if you later want a real, business-audience credential.

videos.title: M4 · The ladder — from a chat you keep repeating to a playbook that runs itself
M4 · Playbook or agent? The honest choice, and why a human still approves
